Lecture 10: Orthogonal Projections ​

Topics: §3.5–3.7 — Inner Product, Orthogonality, Orthogonal Projections (P2=P=PT), Geometric vs Algebraic Characterization, Orthogonal Projection Formula, Diagonal Cross-Filling for Orthonormal Bases Date: Apr 20 – Apr 23, 2026

1. Inner Product and Orthogonality ​

In this lecture, we introduce a special class of projections where the sunlight is perpendicular to the floor. To make this precise, we first need to define what "perpendicular" means algebraically.

1.1 Inner Product (Dot Product) ​

Definition

Inner Product (Dot Product)

For two vectors u,v∈Rn:

u⋅v:=∑i=1nuivi=u1v1+u2v2+⋯+unvn

Equivalently, treating u as a row vector and v as a column vector:

u⋅v=uTv

Example

Example 1.1: Computing Inner Products

Let u=(123) and v=(4−12).

u⋅v=(1)(4)+(2)(−1)+(3)(2)=4−2+6=8

Let w=(2−10).

u⋅w=(1)(2)+(2)(−1)+(3)(0)=2−2+0=0

Proposition

Proposition 1.1: Properties of Inner Product

For vectors u,v,w∈Rn and scalar c:

  1. Symmetry: u⋅v=v⋅u
  2. Linearity: (u+v)⋅w=u⋅w+v⋅w
  3. Homogeneity: (cu)⋅v=c(u⋅v)
  4. Positive definiteness: u⋅u≥0, with equality if and only if u=0

Proof: Direct verification from the definition. ∎

1.2 Orthogonality (Perpendicularity) ​

Definition

Orthogonal Vectors

Two vectors u and v are orthogonal (perpendicular) if:

u⊥v⟺u⋅v=0

We use the notation u⊥v to denote orthogonality.

Remark

Geometric Interpretation

In R2 and R3, orthogonality corresponds to perpendicularity in the usual geometric sense. The algebraic condition u⋅v=0 generalizes this to higher dimensions.

Example

Example 1.2: Orthogonal Vectors

The standard basis vectors in R3 are pairwise orthogonal:

e1=(100),e2=(010),e3=(001)e1⋅e2=0,e1⋅e3=0,e2⋅e3=0

1.3 Orthogonal Subspaces ​

Definition

Orthogonal Subspaces

Two subspaces W and S of Rn are orthogonal (written W⊥S) if every vector in W is orthogonal to every vector in S:

W⊥S⟺w⋅s=0 for all w∈W,s∈S

Example

Example 1.3: Orthogonal Complement from Lecture 6

Recall from Lecture 6 (Four Fundamental Subspaces) that for any matrix A:

Col(AT)⊥Null(A)

This means: every vector in the row space of A is orthogonal to every vector in the null space of A.

2. Orthogonal Projections: Geometric Definition ​

2.1 The Vertical Sunlight Model ​

Recall from Lecture 7 that a general projection P (satisfying P2=P) can be visualized using the sunlight-floor model:

  • Col(P) = the floor
  • Null(P) = direction of sunlight
  • Pv = shadow of v on the floor

For a general projection, the sunlight can shine from any direction. An orthogonal projection is the special case where:

Definition

Orthogonal Projection (Geometric Definition)

A projection P (satisfying P2=P) is called an orthogonal projection if:

Col(P)⊥Null(P)

Geometric interpretation: The sunlight shines perpendicular to the floor.

Example

Example 2.1: Orthogonal vs Oblique Projections in R2

Consider projecting onto the x-axis in R2.

Orthogonal projection: Porth(xy)=(x0)

  • Floor: x-axis (vectors of form (x0))
  • Sunlight direction: y-axis (vectors of form (0y))
  • These are perpendicular ✓

Oblique projection: Poblique(xy)=(x+y0)

  • Floor: x-axis
  • Sunlight direction: direction (1−1)
  • These are not perpendicular ✗

2.2 Why Orthogonal Projections Matter ​

Remark

Why Orthogonal Projections Are Special

Among all projections onto a given subspace W, the orthogonal projection is the unique projection that also satisfies:

Null(P)=W⊥(the orthogonal complement of W)

This makes orthogonal projections the natural choice for:

  • Least squares problems: Finding the best approximation to a vector in a subspace
  • Decomposing vectors: Splitting v=w+s where w∈W, s⊥W
  • Orthonormal bases: Constructing perpendicular coordinate systems

3. Orthogonal Projections: Algebraic Characterization ​

3.1 The Key Question ​

We have defined orthogonal projection geometrically: Col(P)⊥Null(P).

But how do we check this algebraically? Given a matrix P with P2=P, is there a simple matrix equation that tells us whether P is orthogonal?

The answer is beautiful and surprising.

3.2 Symmetric Matrices ​

Definition

Symmetric Matrix

A square matrix A is symmetric if it equals its transpose:

A=AT

Equivalently: aij=aji for all i,j.

Theorem

Theorem 3.1: Geometric Orthogonality ↔ Algebraic Symmetry

Let P be a projection matrix (P2=P). The following are equivalent:

  1. Geometric condition: Col(P)⊥Null(P)
  2. Algebraic condition: P=PT (P is symmetric)

Proof:

We prove both directions.

(2⟹1): Symmetry implies geometric orthogonality

Assume P=PT. We must show: for any v∈Col(P) and w∈Null(P), we have v⋅w=0.

Since v∈Col(P), by Lecture 7 Proposition 3.1: Pv=v.

Since w∈Null(P): Pw=0.

Recall from Lecture 7 Proposition 3.3 (interchanging property):

Null(P)=Col(I−P)

So w=(I−P)u for some u.

Now compute:

v⋅w=vTw=(Pv)T((I−P)u)=vTPT(I−P)u

Using PT=P:

=vTP(I−P)u=vT(P−P2)u

Using P2=P:

=vT(P−P)u=vT0=0

Therefore Col(P)⊥Null(P). ✓

(1⟹2): Geometric orthogonality implies symmetry

Assume Col(P)⊥Null(P), i.e., vTw=0 for all v∈Col(P) and w∈Null(P).

Let ei be the i-th standard basis vector. Then:

  • Pei∈Col(P)
  • (I−P)ej∈Null(P) (by interchanging property)

By the orthogonality assumption:

(Pei)T((I−P)ej)=0

Expanding:

eiTPT(I−P)ej=0

The left side equals the (i,j)-entry of the matrix PT(I−P). Since this holds for all i,j:

PT(I−P)=0

Therefore:

PT=PTP

Taking transpose of both sides:

(PT)T=(PTP)TP=PTP

But we also have PT=PTP from above, so:

P=PT✓

This completes the proof. ∎

::: success Summary: Orthogonal Projection Characterization

An orthogonal projection is a matrix P satisfying two conditions:

  1. Projection property: P2=P
  2. Symmetry: P=PT

These two simple algebraic equations completely capture the geometric idea of "sunlight perpendicular to floor." :::

4. Constructing Orthogonal Projections ​

4.1 Orthogonal Projection Formula ​

Recall from Lecture 9 that we can construct a projection onto W=Col(B) along S=Null(A) using:

P=B(AB)−1A

provided W∩S={0} (equivalently: AB is invertible).

For an orthogonal projection onto W, we want S=W⊥.

From Lecture 6 (Four Fundamental Subspaces), we know:

Null(BT)⊥Col(B)

Therefore, if W=Col(B), then W⊥=Null(BT).

This means: A=BT.

Proposition

Proposition 4.1: Orthogonal Projection Formula

Let B be an n×r matrix with full column rank (rank r). The orthogonal projection onto Col(B) is:

P=B(BTB)−1BT

Proof:

By Lecture 6, Null(BT)=(Col(B))⊥, so A=BT.

By Lecture 9's construction formula:

P=B(AB)−1A=B(BTB)−1BT

We need to verify that BTB is invertible.

Claim: If B has full column rank, then BTB is invertible.

Proof of claim: Suppose (BTB)x=0.

Then:

xT(BTB)x=0(Bx)T(Bx)=0∥Bx∥2=0

Therefore Bx=0.

Since B has full column rank (linearly independent columns), x=0.

By the invertibility criterion: BTB is invertible. ✓

Finally, verify P=PT:

PT=(B(BTB)−1BT)T=B((BTB)−1)TBT=B(BTB)−1BT=P

(using (BTB)T=BTB and ((BTB)−1)T=(BTB)−1)

Therefore P is an orthogonal projection. ∎

Example

Example 4.1: Orthogonal Projection onto a Plane

Find the orthogonal projection matrix onto the plane W=span{(100),(010)} (the xy-plane in R3).

Step 1: B=(100100)

Step 2: BTB=(100010)(100100)=(1001)=I2

Step 3: (BTB)−1=I2

Step 4:

P=B(BTB)−1BT=(100100)I2(100010)=(100010000)

Verification:

  • P2=P ✓
  • PT=P ✓
  • P(xyz)=(xy0) (projects onto xy-plane) ✓

5. Diagonal Cross-Filling for Orthonormal Bases ​

5.1 Motivation: Finding Orthonormal Bases ​

Given an orthogonal projection P, we often want to find an orthonormal basis for Col(P), i.e., basis vectors v1,…,vr such that:

  • vi⋅vj=0 for i≠j (orthogonal)
  • vi⋅vi=1 for all i (unit length)

One approach is to apply diagonal cross-filling to P directly (Lecture 8 method). But there is a more efficient method when P is constructed using the formula P=B(BTB)−1BT.

5.2 Diagonal Cross-Filling of P ​

Proposition

Proposition 5.1: Diagonal Cross-Filling Preserves Orthogonality

Let P=P2=PT be an orthogonal projection. If P≠0, then trace(P)≠0, so P has at least one non-zero diagonal entry.

Let R be a rank-1 matrix obtained by cross-filling P at a diagonal pivot. Then:

  1. R=R2=RT (R is an orthogonal projection)
  2. P−R is an orthogonal projection
  3. rank(P)=rank(R)+rank(P−R)

Proof sketch:

  • Cross-filling at a diagonal pivot preserves symmetry: If P=PT and we cross-fill at (k,k), then R=RT.
  • From Lecture 8: R=R2 and P−R is a projection.
  • Combining: R and P−R are both orthogonal projections. ∎

Remark

Iterative Decomposition

By repeatedly applying diagonal cross-filling, we can write:

P=R1+R2+⋯+Rr

where each Ri is a rank-1 orthogonal projection.

Each Ri can be written as Ri=viviT where vi is a unit vector (since rank(Ri)=trace(Ri)=1 and Ri is orthogonal projection).

The vectors {v1,…,vr} form an orthonormal basis for Col(P).

5.3 Inner Diagonal Cross-Filling: A Clever Shortcut ​

Here comes the clever method: instead of cross-filling the n×n matrix P, we can cross-fill the much smaller r×r matrix (BTB)−1.

Theorem

Theorem 5.1: Inner Diagonal Cross-Filling

Let B be n×r with full column rank, and let P=B(BTB)−1BT be the orthogonal projection onto Col(B).

Suppose we decompose (BTB)−1 using diagonal cross-filling:

(BTB)−1=Q1+Q2+⋯+Qr

where each Qi is rank-1 (from cross-filling at diagonal entries).

Define:

Ri:=BQiBT

Then:

  1. P=R1+R2+⋯+Rr
  2. Each Ri is a rank-1 orthogonal projection
  3. RiRj=0 for i≠j (compatible family)

Proof:

(1):

∑i=1rRi=∑i=1rBQiBT=B(∑i=1rQi)BT=B(BTB)−1BT=P

(2): From Lecture 8, since P=∑Ri is a projection and ∑rank(Ri)≤rank(P), each Ri is automatically a projection.

For symmetry: (BQiBT)T=BQiTBT. Since cross-filling at diagonal preserves symmetry: QiT=Qi. Therefore RiT=Ri.

For rank: Each Qi has rank 1 (by cross-filling). Therefore Ri=BQiBT has rank at most 1. Since Ri≠0 (from non-degenerate cross-filling), rank(Ri)=1.

(3): Compatible family property follows from Lecture 8. ∎

::: success The Beauty of Inner Diagonal Cross-Filling

Standard method (Lecture 8):

  • Cross-fill P (an n×n matrix)
  • Requires n rank-1 decompositions
  • Computational cost: O(n2) per step

Inner diagonal cross-filling:

  • Cross-fill (BTB)−1 (an r×r matrix where r=dim⁡(Col(B)))
  • Requires only r rank-1 decompositions (often r≪n)
  • Computational cost: O(r2) per step
  • Much more efficient when r≪n!

Moreover: The decomposition P=∑BQiBT directly gives us the orthonormal basis vectors from the factorization of each BQiBT. :::

Example

Example 5.1: Inner Diagonal Cross-Filling

Let W=span{(1111),(0122)}.

Step 1:

B=(10111212)

Step 2: Compute BTB:

BTB=(11110122)(10111212)=(4559)

Step 3: Compute (BTB)−1:

det(BTB)=4⋅9−5⋅5=36−25=11(BTB)−1=111(9−5−54)

Step 4: Diagonal cross-filling of (BTB)−1.

Cross-fill at position (1,1) with pivot 9. Use the multiplication table trick: to complete a rank-1 matrix from pivot (1,1), the (2,2) entry must satisfy:

(2,2)=(1,2)×(2,1)(1,1)=(−5)×(−5)9=259

Therefore:

Q1=111(9−5−525/9)

Step 5: Compute Q2=(BTB)−1−Q1:

(BTB)−1−Q1=111(9−5−54)−111(9−5−525/9)=111(0004−25/9)=111(00011/9)=199(00011)

Step 6: Compute R1=BQ1BT.

First compute BQ1:

BQ1=111(10111212)(9−5−525/9)=111(9−54−20/9−15/9−15/9)

Then:

R1=BQ1BT=111(9−54−20/9−15/9−15/9)(11110122)=199(8136−9−93616−4−4−9−411−9−411)

Step 7: Compute R2=BQ2BT.

Since Q2 has zeros except at (2,2):

BQ2=(10111212)⋅199(00011)=19(00010202)

This is 19ve2T where v=(0122).

Therefore:

R2=BQ2BT=19ve2TBT=19vvT=19(0000012202440244)

Step 8: Verify P=R1+R2.

P=B(BTB)−1BT=R1+R2=199(8136−9−936271818−9184545−9184545)

Verification:

  • R2=19vvT where ∥v∥2=0+1+4+4=9, so R2 has rank 1 and trace 19(9)=1 ✓
  • R1=P−R2 is also rank-1 orthogonal projection (by Theorem 5.1) ✓
  • trace(P)=trace(R1)+trace(R2)=1+1=2=rank(P) ✓

Key observation: We decomposed a 4×4 projection into two rank-1 orthogonal projections by only cross-filling a 2×2 matrix!

6. Summary ​

::: success Key Concepts from This Lecture

  1. Inner Product: u⋅v=∑uivi=uTv

  2. Orthogonality: u⊥v⟺u⋅v=0

  3. Orthogonal Projection (two equivalent definitions):

    • Geometric: P2=P with Col(P)⊥Null(P)
    • Algebraic: P2=P and P=PT

  4. Bridge Theorem: Geometric orthogonality ↔ Algebraic symmetry

  5. Orthogonal Projection Formula: P=B(BTB)−1BT

  6. Diagonal Cross-Filling: Decompose P=∑Ri into rank-1 orthogonal projections

  7. Inner Diagonal Cross-Filling: Cross-fill (BTB)−1 instead of P for efficiency

    • Complexity: O(r2) instead of O(n2) where r=dim⁡(Col(B))
    • Automatic: Each Ri=BQiBT is a rank-1 orthogonal projection :::

Looking Ahead ​

In the next lectures (Ch4: Determinants), we will develop tools to:

  • Compute eigenvalues (which generalize the idea of "characteristic directions")
  • Understand the Cayley-Hamilton theorem
  • Spectral decomposition using Lagrange interpolation

The orthogonal projections we studied today will reappear as spectral projections when we study diagonalization of symmetric matrices.

Exercises ​

Problem

Exercise 1: Checking Orthogonality

Determine which pairs of vectors are orthogonal:

(a) u=(123), v=(2−10)

(b) u=(111), v=(1−21)

(c) u=(34), v=(4−3)

Problem

Exercise 2: Verifying Orthogonal Projections

Which of the following are orthogonal projections? (Check both P2=P and P=PT)

(a) P=(1000)

(b) P=(0.50.50.50.5)

(c) P=(0.60.80.80.4)

(d) P=(0.60.8−0.80.6)

Problem

Exercise 3: Orthogonal Projection Formula

Find the orthogonal projection matrix onto:

(a) The line W=span{(34)} in R2

(b) The plane W=span{(101),(011)} in R3

Verify your answer satisfies P2=P and PT=P.

Problem

Exercise 4: Geometric vs Algebraic

Let P=12(110110000).

(a) Verify P is an orthogonal projection (check P2=P and P=PT)

(b) Find bases for Col(P) and Null(P)

(c) Verify geometrically that Col(P)⊥Null(P) by computing inner products of basis vectors

Problem

Exercise 5: Inner Diagonal Cross-Filling

Let B=(100100) (orthogonal projection onto xy-plane).

(a) Compute BTB and (BTB)−1

(b) Apply diagonal cross-filling to (BTB)−1 to write it as Q1+Q2

(c) Compute R1=BQ1BT and R2=BQ2BT

(d) Verify R1+R2=P where P=B(BTB)−1BT

(e) Verify each Ri is a rank-1 orthogonal projection

Problem

Exercise 6: Least Squares Application

Consider the inconsistent system Ax=b where:

A=(101112),b=(088)

(a) Find the orthogonal projection P onto Col(A) using P=A(ATA)−1AT

(b) The least squares solution minimizes ∥b−Ax∥. It satisfies ATAx=ATb. Find it.

(c) Compute Pb and verify it equals Ax where x is the least squares solution
